evidence_scope: sanitized real A/B cohort: same prompt, cheap arm (haiku|low) vs strong arm (opus), objectively graded
method: Three tiers (simple/medium/complex). Both arms received byte-identical prompts except the output file path. Coding tiers were graded by deterministic test scripts with independently verified reference values, not by model self-report. Token counts are total subagent-session tokens (includes fixed harness overhead, so cross-arm token differences are muted; the cost difference comes from per-token price).

pricing_basis.source: Anthropic official model pricing (claude-api skill reference, cached 2026-06-24)

tasks.label: arithmetic: 17*23+5, answer-only, no tools

opus_retries.note: identical prompt retry returned identical token count and identical wrong output '42' — likely a same-(prompt,model) cache replay, counted as 1 distinct attempt
reworded probe to rule out caching

tasks.verdict: haiku PASS, opus FAIL on all distinct attempts (opus computed 17*23 as 383 in the reworded probe; correct is 391)
tasks.status: correctness gate: cheap arm wins outright

tasks.label: merge_overlapping_intervals with touching-interval and unsorted-input edge cases, graded on 6 hand-verified cases
tasks.status: both pass; haiku marginally cheaper in tokens, 16% faster

tasks.label: recursive-descent arithmetic expression evaluator (precedence, unary minus, parens, no eval()), graded on 8 hand-computed cases; both sources inspected for eval()/exec() cheating — clean
tasks.status: both pass; haiku used MORE tokens and time on this tier — reported honestly, not hidden

tokens.note: haiku +0.7% — effectively equal; totals are dominated by fixed per-subagent harness overhead (~15k), so token count is NOT where the saving is

correctness.note: the strong arm failed the SIMPLE tier repeatedly — model price does not guarantee per-task correctness; objective grading is what catches it

aggregate.cost_conclusion: At near-equal token counts, the routed haiku arm costs ~5x less per token (~80% cost reduction) on every task it passes. The boundary-search ledger ensures haiku only keeps task classes it has actually passed; failures escalate one rung and are remembered.

caveats: Single-run pairs, not durable medians — same disclosure Codex's own README makes for its one-run smoke comparison.
Token counts include the fixed subagent system-prompt overhead; marginal generation differences between models are muted at this task size.
The opus simple-tier failure is a real observed result from 2 distinct attempts (plus 1 likely cache replay); it may not reproduce at different times.

Benchmark probes were deliberately NOT recorded into the private adaptive ledger (local/ledger.jsonl), mirroring Codex's rule that synthetic benchmark runs must not calibrate real task-class learning.
